segments.text: Thank you.
Thank you.
If you'd like to be able to listen to the show without ads,
and have full access to bonus content, that's an option.
To find out how, please go to dogmanencounters.com forward slash podcast.
Tonight's guest is Wiley Biro.
Wiley, welcome to the show.
Hey, Vic.
How you doing today?
Oh, fair to Midland.
How about yourself?
I'm doing quite well.
No complaints so far.
Thanks for asking.
Oh, you're welcome.
And that's a great thing.
Wiley, please give us a brief bio on yourself.
Sure, Vic. Not a problem.
My name is Wiley Biro, and I grew up in central New Jersey.
I was born in Princeton, and I grew up in Hillsboro.
Hillsboro basically lands dead smack in the center of New Jersey.
If there was a central Jersey, Hillsboro would be right in the center.
It's kind of an interesting town.
When I was a kid, it was quite rural, mostly farmland, some suburban communities.
But it was pretty much on the border of like the highly developed New Jersey that goes north into New York City.
That like super developed part of New Jersey basically stops at my town.
So like the northern side of my town is very developed.

And then when you get towards the northwestern side, it becomes more rural and you hit the Sourland Mountain Preserve. And I grew up on a street that was like right at the top of the Sourland Mountain Preserve, really beautiful property my parents had surrounded by farmland and forest.
So, I, you know, growing up in one of the most densely populated states per capita,
I was lucky enough to, you know, be surrounded by the outdoors.
It was like a little paradise in New Jersey.
So, I didn't have like the typical childhood growing up in New Jersey.
I didn't have neighbors and kids all around.
It was basically just me and my folks.
And ever since I was a little baby, my mom, before I could even walk, would throw me into
her backpack.
of those little baby backpacks that you could hike around with. And she would take me hiking
all through the mountains and the trails just as a kid. And, you know, I really grew up,
I grew up just exploring in the forest. I had a lot of freedom as a kid. My mom would just
let me run outside. We did have a rule though. If my mom ever whistled or called for me,
I had to come right away. So, you know, she had me trained really well. And, you know,
I never ran into issues or anything like that.
But as a kid, my town in Hillsboro was very rural.
There was one traffic light in the town at Amwell and 206.
And that was the only traffic light in the town.
Today, most of the farmland and a lot of the open space has been developed.
Now there's a traffic light practically on every single street corner.

So it really this town has changed so much within 36 years I actually turned 36 November 22nd of last month And I actually just moved back to New Jersey from Oregon and Washington I been living between the both states for the past year I was a wildland forest firefighting out there which was quite incredible To back up a little bit so I grew up in Hillsboro I went to the school systems here in Hillsboro I graduated high school in 2008 High school in New Jersey everyone
was really preppy. Everyone had like Abercrombie and Fitch. And I was sort of like one of the
a little bit of an outcast. I was never really bullied in high school. I had plenty of friends,
I was really into the outdoors.
In my senior year of high school, I founded the first outdoor survival program in high school.
That was a really great experience.
The club actually still exists today.
That was really fun.
After high school, I didn't know what I really wanted to do with life.
I went to a wilderness survival school.
It wasn't like any survival school.
This was an expensive, prestigious survival school where after completing a year-long course, they would certify you to be an outdoor survival instructor.
And the school was located in Duval, Washington.
So at 18 years old, I got shipped across the country and dropped off 10 miles up the foothills of the Cascade Mountains to go to this survival school.
And it was an amazing experience.
And I became really comfortable in the outdoors.
I learned to track animals. I learned about botany and ecology and what plants were edible and which plants are medicinal.
And I learned how to, you know, do hunting in a primitive style.
And it was just like an incredible experience.
I learned every tree species and plant species in the Pacific Northwest.
And I just really loved that experience.
I got to do intensive camping all throughout Washington State, Oregon, California.
And, oh, man, that experience just really gave me this confidence that I never would have gained otherwise.
A confidence that, you know, if stuff hits the fan and I have to go off grid, I will be in a better position than a lot of other people.
So with that said, you know, after the program, I ended up moving back to New Jersey and I worked at Starbucks briefly.
I became the assistant manager and then I decided that I want to do more traveling.
And I looked into this program called AmeriCorps and it was a really cool program because you get to try all different types of jobs and travel all over the country helping people.

So I ended up doing that for two years, which was another really great, amazing experience. I really got to learn so many different trades. I did carpentry. I became certified as a wildland forest firefighter through that program. I also got certified through FEMA to do disaster relief.

So I was sent all over the country. Anytime there was a natural disaster, I got to go to Alabama because there were tornadoes. I got to go into Texas due to some flooding. Then I got to do wildland forest firefighting all over the South. I was in Virginia, North Carolina, New Jersey, New York, all really over the West Coast doing firefighting, which was really fun.
And I still hadn't gone to college yet.
But while I was firefighting, I met a bunch of wildlife biologists that were just so passionate about saving wildlife and giving back to nature.
And some of these people that I was getting to work with were so inspiring to me.
And like through my outdoor background, I realized like one of my passions is, you know, just giving back to nature and doing what I can to make the forest healthier.
Because the human race, we tend to take and take and take from nature.
And I really noticed in this life, if I could give back, I feel like I'll live a good life.
So that's sort of been one of my goals whenever I'm looking for work and just in life.
I love going out into the forest, hiking, mushroom hunting, camping.
and uh yeah so after AmeriCorps I um ended up basically moving back to the Pacific Northwest
I was living in Washington state I gained residency then I um went to the Evergreen
State College and I studied wildlife biology and basically forest management um and uh
And I got my degree and I ended up moving back to New Jersey again.
And I started doing tree work because it was just a job I had landed.
And it was a really interesting job because it was somewhat science-based.
When I got hired, I learned to climb trees and prune trees properly.
And then I also got into the plant health care aspect where I was going around and
treating plants and trees for certain diseases.
And I also did a bunch of invasive species removal projects.
So after being an arborist for five years in New Jersey I really got burnt out on living in New Jersey And just being like this job really just made me feel kind of stuck
So I just manifested moving back to Oregon, to the Pacific Northwest.
So this was about a year ago.
I moved back to the Pacific Northwest as an arborist.
But I knew that when I got back to the Pacific Northwest, I wanted to do firefighting.
firefighting because that's a really good way to help the forest and help people.
And it was just like something I've always wanted to get back into.
So a year ago, I moved back.
I moved into Portland.
I did tree work for a couple months.
Then I found a job as a wildland forest firefighter.
And that's what I continued to do the rest of the year in Washington and Oregon, basically.
But I was based out of Salem, Oregon.
So all summer I was out in the mountains fighting fires, running chainsaws, and just doing everything I can to basically save the forest out there.
And it was an incredible experience.
So right now it's the off season.
I'm not doing any firefighting.
I'm still on call for any disaster reliefs that happen in the country.
And in fact, right now I just want to put my best wishes out there to everyone in the Pacific Northwest.
And I just hope everyone's safe because there's major flooding going on right now.
It's super unprecedented.
So my heart goes out to everyone out in Pacific Northwest right now.
I was actually in Washington State one week ago.
So I missed that storm by a week.
I actually just moved back to New Jersey a few days ago.
So that's basically a little brief bio on myself.
Vic. Holy cow, Wiley. You sure have lived an exciting life. Oh, yeah. Thank you so much. Yeah,
I try to not stay stagnant. I really love adventures and I love the outdoors. And yeah,
I just I like to I'd like to do different things and try different jobs. Yeah. So thanks, Vic.
Oh, no, you're welcome. Yeah, they can make a movie about you about that life you've lived.
Yeah, maybe.
They could.
And speaking of work, from what I understand, you're looking for employment right now.
Would you like to put a plug out for that?
Oh, sure, Vic.
That would be incredible.
Yeah, so I am a certified wildland forest firefighter, and I'm unemployed right now,
but I'm looking for different work, something a little less physically vigorous and a little safer.
But I'm in the field of forest management and wildlife biology.
And yeah, if anyone knows any positions available, I'm flexible and willing to move.
And yeah, I have an amazing sense of the outdoors as far as reading the landscape, tracking animals.
And yeah, I'm a very hard worker doing tree work and firefighting.
I'm happy to work long, long hours.
And there's no job that I'm not afraid to do.
So if anyone out there has any jobs in mind, please keep me in mind.
So thank you for that, Vic.
Oh, you're welcome.
Glad to do it.

That'll make it really easy for you to reach him.
If you've had a dogman encounter that you'd like to speak with me about, whether in private or on the show, please go to dogmanencounters.com and submit a report.
Let's get into your encounters now. Please give us every last detail that comes to mind.
Okay, Vic. So, I'm going to rewind a couple weeks before the encounter just to give some background that leads us up to the situation.
so basically about two weeks maybe three weeks before this encounter this situation happened
I was on a camping trip in Florida I did a 17 day long camping trip I was working as an arborist at
the time I'd been working for about three years and I had a bunch of vacation time saved up so
we were coming close to January and my vacation hours weren't going to roll over so if I didn't
use the vacation time, I'd lose it. So I took full advantage of that. And I went on a 17-day
camping trip in Florida. So I had a partner at the time. And this girl was like the love of my life.
I wanted to marry her. I wanted to have a family with her. And that was our plan.
So she was about seven months pregnant at this time. And I was under the assumption that it was
my boy It was supposed to be a boy and I was expecting a son Going on this camping trip she completely encouraged you know she was living her parents lived right across the street So
she was totally fine. She worked from home. And so, it wasn't really a big problem for me to go
on this vacation. And she was totally encouraging of it. But like, I don't know, 14 days into the
vacation, she called me and told me that she was done. And she moved me out of the house,
which caught me off guard because the day before, she left me a message, you know,
telling me that the baby was kicking. And anyway, to make a long story short,
I was living with her and I hadn't lived at my folks house in probably 10 years. So,
I was 32 at this time and she broke up with me, moved all my stuff out of our apartment while I
was on vacation into my parents' house. Didn't give me any reason why. Obviously, I was trying
to do everything I could to fix this because I was, you know, thinking we were going to have a
kid. And so, like, I ended up moving back in with my parents and it was like, I was so depressed and
I felt so defeated and I was heartbroken and I didn't understand what was going on because I had
done everything in this relationship. I had already set up a whole nursery for the child.
I had time taken off from work. I had family members sending me baby gifts and my main
employer had given me all this stuff from their baby who was just born or who was born a few,
a couple years earlier. And so, like, when this happened, it was a huge embarrassment.
But after I moved back in with my parents, the girl was still having me come over and cook dinner
and do all this stuff for her. And she really was just taking complete advantage of me.
So, one day, I was at my parents' house feeling really depressed, hoping that
there was a chance that we would get back together. And she called me one night and said that,
yeah, I decided I don't want the baby to have the name you chose and your name's not going to be on
the birth certificate. And I was like, what? That's extremely hurtful. And then she unfessed
up and said, I'm so sorry, it's not yours. And a bunch of red flags came up during that time
because when I thought about it, it kind of made sense because, yeah, I don't need to go into more
details, but it ended up not being mine. And I was very upset. My family was upset and disappointed.
You know, so many years later now, like, I realized that the whole situation was a blessing
in disguise. I wouldn't have been happy in that relationship. And it would have, you know,
it never would have worked. It wasn't my kid anyway. So, I'm at my parents' house and I'm
really mourning this whole situation. It was really hard for my parents to accept. At the time,
A few weeks after the fact, they totally were on my side and were happy that the situation happened the way it did because they really never liked the girl at all.
My parents' property, like I said earlier in the interview, is kind of in a rural area of New Jersey.
We're surrounded by farmland and forest.
This is my home.
I grew up hiking out here.
I know every square inch of the forest behind my house.
I've hiked all in the farmland and all the forests around the farmland
and I really feel comfortable out here like it's my second home
and you know after living in Washington state for a long time
the forests out there are so dense and expansive and
you know they can really give you a foreboding feeling when you're out there
so like you know coming out here I just feel like
I'm kind of like top predator king of the forest and
I was having one of those morning moments, like a morning situation, like I was upset
and I wasn't thinking rationally at all. And typically when I go into the forest,
I keep a clear head. Being out in the forest for me is like, gives me a sense of peace.
And that's one of the reasons why I love hiking. And typically when I'm in the forest,
I have like a really calm, collected mind setting.
But in this situation, I was crushed.
I was heartbroken.
And I really went outside.
Like in a bludgeoning state, I wasn't thinking rationally.
I went out without a flashlight.
So my parents, on one side of the road is, I live on East Mountain Road.
And on one side of the road is the Sauerland Mountains.
And basically, you cross the road from my parents' house,
and it goes straight up a hill into the mountains.
And it's all forested.
And there's mountain-esque features.
It's a tiny, it's not even a real mountain.
It's more of a hill.
But there's a lot of mountain-esque features like giant boulders and there's like spring-fed creeks.

And one of the creeks is called the Roaring Rocks because like when you find the mouth of the creek kind of near where this – you cross over the street from my parents' house and you could see the creek.
And you follow the creek and the rocks in the creek get bigger and bigger and bigger the further up the hill you get until you can't see the creek anymore.
But you could hear the creek under the rocks.
So, they call it the Rumbling Rocks.
Um, so a lot of these like really special, like mountain-esque features are off trail.
And a lot of the people, there's hiking trails all around there.
There's a park.
And a lot of the people that go to the hike up at the Sourlands don't know of these like
secret spots with giant boulders.
And there's this one spot called Devil's Half Acre and there's no trails that lead to it.
I feel like I'm probably one of the few people that actually know where Devil's Half Acre
um so it's not that far from my parents house like you cross the street and like five six seven
minutes walking up into the forest you maybe 10 minutes and you're at devil's half acre which is
this boulder field in the middle of the forest where trees can't really grow in the middle
because the boulders block the trees from growing and there's all these little crevices and caves
that you could crawl into. And there's definitely animals living there. It's a really interesting
place. Every time I go there during the day, I see vultures. So, there's like a vulture nest
that lives there. Every time I've gone there, I've seen a vulture. It's really strange. And
one of the last times I went there, I saw baby vultures, which was really kind of cool to see.
They were super cute, baby vultures. Yeah, I've never seen baby vultures before.
but there's all types of animals that den up in these like rock crevices um and you could some

to get back to the story, I'm really upset. And I just, it's nighttime. And I decided to go

I didn't have any weapons, no self-defense. It was just the clothes on my back and me.
So I'm going out into the forest at night and I cross the street and I'm making my way over

kind of one of those moments I'm sure all of us have had in our lives. And I was just thinking,
like, I also like, I don't know, I just got this feeling like, oh, am I disrupting the forest?

And then I got this egotistical feeling like, no, I'm not disrupting the forest. In fact, this is my forest. I grew up here. And I was just being loud. And all of a sudden, I heard a growl, a low, low growl.
And, uh, it sounded like it was canine. And, but when it happened, um, my heart dropped into my stomach, became a little tiny pit. And instantly I knew that it was something, um, unusual.

I also, I knew immediately just my intuition, like something in the back of my head, as if I was looking at the creature, which I wasn't, I couldn't see anything, which was even scarier because like I'm hanging out in these, in this forest by myself, no flashlight, and I'm being loud and obnoxious.
and something started growling at me and it was a low foreboding growl. And when you're in the
forest alone with no flashlight and something growls at you, it's extremely unsettling.

I did. So I was like, okay, it's time to get out of here. And let me remind you, I don't have a

I've seen mountain lions in the wild, and I've seen coyotes and wolves. I've actually seen a
